symbols: garden and fountain — Susanna's private space of innocence violated by corrupt power
Susanna's nakedness — vulnerability of the righteous before unjust accusers
two elders in rich robes — the corruption of religious authority and abuse of judicial power
dark trees and shadows — concealment, scheming, and the hidden nature of the elders' sin
distant classical building — the civic order that the elders' corruption undermines
stone fountain with carved figure — domestic order and beauty threatened by predatory intrusion
description: In a shaded garden before a stone fountain, a young woman sits partially nude, her lower body draped in white and blue cloth. She turns with surprise and alarm toward two elderly men who have emerged from behind the trees. One elder, in a brown robe, leans forward aggressively while the other, wearing a red turban and elaborate pink silk robe, stands upright gesturing toward her. Dense foliage and dark trees frame the scene, with a classical building visible in the distant left background. A heron or crane perches on an element at far right.
theological_description: The story of Susanna (Daniel 13 in the Septuagint/Vulgate tradition) is a paradigmatic narrative about the persecution of the innocent and God's vindication of the righteous. The two elders, appointed as judges, abuse their sacred office to prey upon Susanna, threatening to falsely accuse her of adultery if she refuses their advances. Susanna chooses death over sin, crying out to God who alone knows the truth (Daniel 13:42-43). The young Daniel, inspired by the Holy Spirit, exposes the elders' lie through cross-examination, and Susanna is vindicated. Patristic exegesis, particularly Hippolytus of Rome, read Susanna as a type of the Church — the chaste bride persecuted by corrupt powers but ultimately delivered by God. The garden setting echoes Eden: a place of beauty corrupted by sin. The narrative also prefigures Christ's own trial before corrupt judges and His vindication through resurrection. The story powerfully affirms that God hears the cry of the innocent and judges the wicked, even when earthly justice fails.

scripture_refs.reason: The elders spy on Susanna bathing in her garden and confront her with their demand — the scene depicted
Susanna cries out to God who hears her prayer and raises up Daniel to vindicate her
Daniel cross-examines the elders, exposes their lie, and they are condemned to death
The Lord loves justice and will not forsake His saints — divine vindication of the innocent
God hates a false witness who breathes out lies — the elders' false testimony against Susanna
